nativeRange.habitat: The Blue Reef Chromis inhabits outer reef faces and drop-offs throughout its range, typically at depths of 3 to 60 m, with the greatest concentrations between 10 and 20 m. Groups congregate in mid-water above coral formations, picking zooplankton from the current.

diet.notes: Primarily planktivorous in the wild, with documented consumption of copepods, tunicates, small crustaceans, and fish eggs. In captivity tends to accept a variety of meaty preparations (mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood), herbivore flakes, and pellets. Multiple small feedings daily are generally advised to mimic natural grazing behavior. Vitamin-enriched foods have been reported to help maintain vibrant coloration.

summary: A vivid, electric-blue schooling damselfish from the Caribbean that is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly saltwater fish. Tends to be peaceful toward non-conspecifics and is widely regarded as reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Blue Reef Chromis (Chromis cyanea) is native to the Western Atlantic, ranging from Bermuda and southern Florida through the Caribbean Sea, the Bahamas, the Gulf of Mexico, and the Antilles. In the wild it inhabits outer reef faces at depths of 10–20 m, congregating in mid-water aggregations above coral structure.

The species is generally considered beginner-friendly, though juveniles and newly imported specimens can be delicate and may require careful acclimation. Adults reach 10–15 cm in length. The older scientific name Azurina cyanea appears in some references; Chromis cyanea (Poey, 1860) is the currently accepted name.

Conspecific dynamics deserve attention. Adult males tend to be highly territorial toward one another, with documented territories of 3–20 m² in the wild. Groups of juveniles generally school more cohesively, but in smaller aquariums dominance hierarchies can emerge, with larger individuals harassing subordinates. Keeping groups of six or more in a suitably large tank — at least 300 L for the group — has been reported to distribute aggression more broadly and reduce stress on subordinate fish. Keeping a single specimen or a very small group in an undersized tank may lead to chronic stress for subordinate individuals.

Unlike many reef fish, the Blue Reef Chromis does not change sex. The species is gonochoristic, meaning males and females are fixed at birth, and no sequential sex change has been documented.

Spawning, when it occurs, follows a monthly lunar cycle. Males perform elaborate signal-jump and zig-zag courtship displays to attract females, then guard and aerate adhesive benthic eggs — deposited on hard substrate — as the sole parental caregivers. Breeding in captivity has been reported, but successful rearing of larvae is considered difficult given the larvae's extremely small size and specialized feeding requirements.
breedingNotes: Oviparous and likely to spawn on a monthly lunar cycle in well-maintained reef aquariums, though successful rearing of larvae in captivity has been reported rarely. Males establish and defend territories, performing signal-jump displays to attract females. Eggs are adhesive and deposited on hard substrate; the male guards and aerates them until hatching. Larvae are pelagic and extremely small, making captive rearing challenging. Breeding difficulty is rated difficult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm TL max), temperature range (25.6–28.1°C preferred), native range (Western Atlantic/Caribbean), diet (zooplankton/copepods), and taxonomy. FishBase uses the synonym Azurina cyanea in some records.

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (avg 13 cm, max 15 cm), temperature (21–27°C), lifespan (exceeds 5 years captive), diet composition (52% copepods, 34% tunicates), and reproductive biology (lunar spawning, male parental care, gonochoristic).

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked care level (easy), temperament (peaceful), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), minimum tank (30 gal). Agrees with LiveAquaria on most parameters; recommends groups of 6+ in larger tanks.

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm), minimum tank (300 L / 79 gal — notably higher than LiveAquaria's 30 gal; tank size disagreement documented in careNotes), grouping notes (1 male to 5+ females), and acclimation sensitivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous; lunar-synchronized monthly spawning. Males guard and aerate adhesive benthic eggs. Captive rearing of larvae is rarely successful due to extremely small larval size and specialized feeding requirements.
